g.data-narration: Welcome to Android Architecture! We'll explore the five-layer stack that powers billions of devices worldwide.
Understanding Android's layered architecture helps developers optimize performance and understand how apps interact with hardware.
g.text: • Know how apps interact with hardware
Each layer provides services to the layer above
and uses services from the layer below - enabling

g.data-narration: Android has five main layers: System Apps, Application Framework, Native Libraries and ART, HAL, and the Linux Kernel.
The Linux Kernel forms Android's foundation, providing hardware drivers, process management, memory management, and security.
g.text: Android uses a modified Linux kernel as its foundation, providing
core system services like hardware abstraction, security, and process
management. It's open-source and proven in millions of devices.

g.data-narration: The kernel handles hardware drivers, process scheduling, memory allocation, power management, security permissions, and networking protocols.
The HAL provides standard interfaces between the Java framework and hardware drivers, exposing device capabilities to higher layers.
g.text: HAL provides standard interfaces that expose device hardware capabilities
to the Java API framework. It consists of library modules, each implementing
an interface for specific hardware like camera, audio, sensors, and graphics.

g.data-narration: HAL modules include Camera HAL, Audio HAL, Bluetooth HAL, Sensors HAL, and Graphics HAL - each connecting to specific kernel drivers.
ART replaced Dalvik in Android 5.0, using AOT compilation to convert bytecode to native machine code at install time for better performance.
g.text: ART replaced Dalvik starting Android 5.0 (Lollipop). It uses Ahead-of-Time
(AOT) compilation to convert bytecode to native machine code at install time,
significantly improving runtime performance over the older Dalvik VM.

g.data-narration: ART compiles at install time for faster app startup and better battery life, while Dalvik used JIT and had slower performance.
Android includes native libraries like WebKit, OpenGL, SQLite, and Media framework, providing core functionality written in C and C++.
g.text: These core libraries written in C/C++ power Android's fundamental capabilities:

g.data-narration: The Application Framework provides Java/Kotlin APIs that developers use to build apps, including Activity Manager, Window Manager, and more.
g.text: The framework provides high-level Java/Kotlin APIs that developers use to
build Android applications. These building blocks expose core Android capabilities
like lifecycle management, UI components, and data sharing between apps.

g.data-narration: Key framework services include Activity Manager for lifecycle, Window Manager for display, Content Providers for data sharing, and View System for UI.
The top layer contains system apps like Phone and Camera, plus user-installed apps from Google Play, each in isolated sandboxes.
g.text: System apps (Phone, Contacts, Camera, Browser) are pre-installed and can be
used by other apps. User apps run in isolated processes with unique Linux
user IDs for security - each app gets its own sandbox.

g.data-narration: When you take a photo, data flows from the Camera app through the framework, HAL, kernel, and to the camera hardware.
g.text: Data Flow Example: Taking a Photo
Image data flows back up through the layers to display in the app

g.data-narration: Android architecture has five layers: Kernel provides foundation, HAL bridges hardware, ART executes code, Framework enables development, and Apps deliver functionality.
g.text: • Linux Kernel: Foundation with drivers, security,
• HAL: Standard interfaces between framework and hardware
• Data Flow: Apps → Framework → HAL → Kernel
• Each layer provides services to layer above
• Hardware flows back up to the app
Understanding this stack is essential for Android development!
